stem: A team configures their support agent to "escalate after three failed tool calls." What is wrong with this rule?
options: Three is too few — five is the industry standard
Escalation should never be automatic
The category and impact of the failure matter more than the count — one uncertain-state write may demand immediate escalation while five transient read timeouts demand none
Failed tool calls cannot be counted reliably
rationales: No count is the right count — the dimension itself is wrong.
Escalation should absolutely be systematic; it just needs the right triggers.

Escalate on the nature of the situation: user asks for a human, authority is lacking, policy exceptions arise, progress stalls, or tool results show unsafe/uncertain state. A counter is blind to all of that.
Counting is easy; it just measures the wrong thing.

stem: What should an escalation handoff to a human agent contain?
options: Structured context: customer ID, issue type, root cause, relevant record IDs, amounts, actions already taken, and a recommended next action
The user's first complaint message, verbatim
The complete conversation transcript
Just the case number — humans prefer to start fresh
rationales: The human should start where the agent stopped, not where the customer started. Structure makes the context actionable in seconds.
The first complaint omits everything learned since.
Transcript dumps work only when the receiving system can actually use them — usually they bury the signal.
Starting fresh forces the customer to repeat everything — the exact failure users hate most.

stem: A frustrated user demands a human, but the agent can resolve the issue immediately. What is the right behavior?
options: Transfer immediately — the user's request overrides everything
Resolve it silently, then tell them it's fixed
Ask a series of intake questions to document frustration before transfer
Acknowledge the frustration and offer both: "I can resolve this now, and I can also transfer you if you prefer" — preserving their choice
rationales: Transferring away from an instant fix serves the process, not the person — offer the fix while honoring the preference.
Silently acting after someone asked for a human overrides their stated choice.
An intake questionnaire is friction stacked on frustration.
Acknowledge, then present the immediate resolution alongside the transfer option. The user stays in control either way.

stem: Which enforcement mechanism is appropriate for "refunds above $500 require manager approval"?
options: A prominent system prompt rule with IMPORTANT markers
Tool-level enforcement, middleware, permissions, or hooks — prompt instructions guide behavior but are not tamper-proof
Few-shot examples of correctly refusing large refunds
Post-hoc anomaly detection on refund logs
rationales: Prompt language biases the agent; adversarial users and injected content can push past it.
Hard rules — regulated workflows, thresholds, destructive operations — are enforced in code. The prompt can explain the policy; the tool must enforce it.
Examples improve typical behavior, not worst-case behavior — and policy is about the worst case.
Detection finds violations after the money moved.

stem: In the threshold-enforcement pattern, where does the refund limit VALUE come from, and what happens when a call exceeds it?
options: From the system prompt; excess calls fail silently
From a model-passed parameter; excess calls are logged
From a server-controlled source — feature flag, policy service, account record — and excess calls return a structured `requires_approval` result that routes to a manager
From the tool description; excess calls throw exceptions
rationales: Prompts state policy; they can't be the source of truth for enforcement values.
Anything the model passes, the model controls — the threshold must live where the model can't touch it.
No override parameter exists on the public interface, the limit lives server-side, and over-limit requests become pending approvals rather than silent failures. Every property is load-bearing.
Descriptions inform selection; they're not configuration. And silent exceptions strand the workflow.

stem: In preview-then-execute for high-impact actions, what makes the one-time token actually safe?
options: It is encrypted with the server's private key

It is short-lived and bound to the previewed payload — the model cannot construct one from scratch or reuse one with different parameters, so execution can only follow a real preview the user confirmed
It is logged for audit purposes
It is generated by the model so the model stays accountable
rationales: The cryptography matters less than the binding: token ↔ exact previewed action.
The token's constraints ARE the mechanism: no token without a preview, no reuse, no parameter swapping. Execution is structurally downstream of user confirmation.
Logging observes; it doesn't prevent.
A model-generated token defeats the entire point — the model could then skip the preview.

stem: "The model already checked the policy before calling the tool, so the tool skips authorization." What is the correct assessment?
options: Acceptable if the policy check is in the system prompt
Acceptable for read-only tools
Efficient — double-checking wastes latency
Wrong — tools live inside the trust boundary and must re-verify the caller's authority on every invocation; "the model already checked" is not a defense
rationales: Prompt-level checks are advisory; they can't stand in for server-side authorization.
Even reads can leak data the caller isn't authorized to see.
An authorization check is microseconds; an unauthorized state change is an incident.
The model is outside the trust boundary — well-behaved or not. Server-side validation on every state change is non-negotiable.

stem: What does defense-in-depth look like for a customer service agent's policy rules?
options: Prompt rules to bias the agent toward policy, tool implementations to enforce it, and audit logs to detect what slipped through — each layer doing the job the others can't
Three increasingly strict system prompts checked in sequence
One perfectly written enforcement layer, tested exhaustively
Human review of every agent action
rationales: Bias, enforce, detect. Prompts make compliance the agent's default; tools make violations impossible; audits catch the unforeseen. Removing any layer leaves a specific gap.
Three prompts are one layer repeated — injection that beats one beats all three.
Perfection isn't available; detection exists because enforcement has bugs.
Reviewing everything doesn't scale and still doesn't prevent — it detects with maximal cost.

stem: Midway through resolving a shipping claim, the refund tool goes down, though the agent has verified eligibility. What should the agent tell the user?
options: "We hit an error. Please try again later."
Nothing yet — wait for the tool to recover
"Your refund is being processed" — it will likely succeed on retry
What's done, what's pending, and how to finish: eligibility is verified, the refund submission is temporarily unavailable, and here are the options — retry shortly, escalate, or get notified
rationales: A generic error discards the real progress made and tells the user nothing actionable.
Silence reads as abandonment mid-issue.
Claiming a side effect that hasn't happened is the cardinal sin — users tolerate visible incompleteness, not discovering a "completed" action silently never ran.
Partial completion done right: verified facts stated, the gap named honestly, concrete next steps offered.

stem: The same tool keeps failing on the same input, attempt after attempt. What should the agent do?
options: Keep retrying — persistence eventually wins
Treat repeated same-input failure as a signal to switch strategies: try a different tool, ask a clarifying question, or escalate
Report the tool as permanently broken
Silently skip that part of the task
rationales: Identical input, identical failure — more attempts buy nothing.
Repetition is information: this path doesn't work. Strategy changes — different tool, more information from the user, or a human — are what progress looks like now.
One input failing doesn't condemn the tool for other inputs.
Silent gaps surface later as broken promises.

stem: The notification service hasn't confirmed sending the customer's confirmation email. What may the agent say?
options: Only what is true: the request was submitted but delivery isn't confirmed — never claim a side effect happened when the system hasn't completed it
"Your confirmation email has been sent" — it almost certainly will be
"Check your inbox in a few minutes"
Nothing about the email at all
rationales: Honest uncertainty preserves trust and prevents the user from acting on a false premise.
"Almost certainly" is exactly the gap where trust dies.
Implies the send is a done deal — same claim, softer wording.
Omission leaves the user wondering whether to expect anything; the honest partial answer is better.
sourceRef: ## 9 > Graceful Degradation (don't claim uncompleted side effects)
